data.title: Comstock's Moonshot Businesses Should Be Heavily Discounted Based On Prior Dismal Execution
data.description:
Comstock Inc. (NYSE:LODE) is a former mining exploration company that has recently pivoted to several unrelated, in-development clean energy projects related to solar panel recycling and biofuels production, among others.

The company generates little revenue, which cannot nearly cover its operational expenses. Those expenses will probably need to expand in order to accommodate the large costs of developing three separate businesses.

In terms of execution, coming from decades of failed business projects, I doubt the company's ability to bring these new complex projects to profitability.

The company's financial situation is not bad, but only at the expense of dilution. Future operations will need more dilution still.

For these reasons, I do not believe Comstock is an attractive investment and initiate coverage of the name with a Hold rating.

A Former Miner

If we observe Comstock's filings up to FY19 (10-K), the company presented itself as a gold mining exploration business. The company had developed exploration rights in the Comstock, Nevada district (from where its name comes) since 2003, according to that filing.

In that capacity, the company managed to produce a small amount of gold from tail processing (that is, residues from previous mining activities) between 2012 and 2017, after which part of the project was abandoned and part sold (see FY15 and FY18 10-Ks under the MD&A sections).

By FY20, the company referred to itself as an "emerging leader in climate-smart mineral development and production of increasingly scarce strategic and precious metals" Since then, the company has maintained the mining exploration rights (unexploited) under its Comstock Mining segment but has added other businesses.

Developing Several Complex Businesses

When we look at the company's portfolio of businesses as of October 2025 (latest presentation, page 22), we find two core businesses and several investments, besides the exploration rights in Nevada.

The first one is a solar panel recycling business, under the Comstock Minerals segment. The company calls this business "urban mining", but the reality is that it has nothing to do with mining. It is a recycling operation, which currently has the capacity to process approximately 135 thousand panels per year, or a less than industrial-scale operation (see presentation). The company says in the presentation that it expects to eventually reach a processing capacity of 10 million panels per year.

In this business, we already find the two characteristics that make up the other businesses in Comstock's portfolio. First, the company has no experience in the field, coming from mining exploration. Second, the scale of production is only experimental, not industrial.

The second business is a biofuel experimental business called Bioleum. The business is trying to produce biofuels from lignin, the biopolymer inside most wood. This is different from producing biofuels from crops like corn and sugarcane, as is done today.

It also participates in Green Li-Ion, a battery recycling development company, again, a complex technical procedure in the development stage. The company also has participations in real estate businesses (Comstock Real Estate and Sierra Springs Opportunity Funds), which hold land in Sierra Springs, Nevada, a "census-designated place"(not even called a town by the US census) of 5,300 people. Green Li-Ion and the real estate businesses are not consolidated (see 2Q25 10-Q under Long-Term Investments).

No profitability in sight

The company generates very little revenue, almost entirely from the plant it has for solar recycling (see Segment Reporting on the latest 10-Q).

This level of operations is not even close to covering the large OpEx expenses the company posts, for about $37 million on a TTM basis (versus $3.3 million in revenues).

Financing is OK but Requires Dilution

The company has maintained a relatively clean balance sheet, without much debt, and where liquidity risk is not that high, but only at the expense of massive dilution.

Since 2020, shares outstanding have multiplied by 25 times, meaning a shareholder from that period has been diluted to 4% of the company today.

As of 2Q25, the company had $18 million in cash listed above, plus $30 million from a recent financing obtained in August (therefore not reflected above) in exchange for 13 million shares (meaning an additional 26% dilution).

This cash should allow the company to invest in expanding its solar panel recycling operations and also to sustain its operational expenses.

In addition, the company has recently sold preferred shares in its biofuels business, which will be deconsolidated after FY25 (see 2Q25 earnings report), meaning additional savings of approximately $15 million in OpEx (half of the company's yearly figure) based on 2Q25 data.

That is, the company could potentially consolidate operational losses of about $20 million (from the $37 million today), which, compared to the $48 million in cash, should not represent a liquidity problem, unless the company has aggressive capital allocation plans.

Not Attractive in Execution and Complexity

Comstock claims that its operations will be very profitable in the future, but it has basically said so of all of its businesses.

On the October 2025 presentation, it claims that its mining properties have a 10% discounted NPV of $260 million, even though it has failed to generate any meaningful revenues from them in 20+ years.

It also claims, on that same presentation (page 11), that the economics of a 100 thousand ton panel recycling operation (equivalent to 10 million panels per year capacity, versus 132 thousand panels today) can generate a return of about $55 million per year, with a capital expenditure of only $12 million. This contradicts the majority of the general knowledge on economics and investment by revealing a 500% return on capital investment with no IP protection, which yet appears to be uncompetitive.

Finally, it claimed in its April 2025 presentation (page 15) that its Bioleum project could potentially generate gross profits of between $3.4 and $7 per gallon of fuel, versus $0.4 for regular gasoline.

These are obviously potential scenarios, based on management's best estimates, but I could also have good-faith estimates of how much gold I can find on Jupiter, and that would not get me an inch closer to those profits.

The company is addressing several complex technical markets at the same time, which require OpEx and capital investments, without any experience in those fields and with a track record of dismal execution in previous businesses.

Therefore, those profits have to be heavily discounted compared to the much more real scenario of a company that continues to keep high OpEx expenses that need to be financed by consistent dilution of 95%+ since 2020 alone.

If we consider the company's peak price, close to its IPO, the company has reached a 90%+ decrease twice, first from $750 to $75 and then from $75 to $3.4 today.

For these reasons, I do not think Comstock's businesses, all in development and in complex areas, should be considered profitable in the very near or even distant future. Without profits, there are no cash flows to equity, and therefore, the equity should be worth very little, if anything.

With that, I cannot consider Comstock's stock attractive and initiate with a Hold rating on the name. I do not issue a Sell rating because Comstock's liquidity has been fairly low in the past (500 thousand shares at $3 is about $1.5 million, or less than 1% of the company's market cap today), which can lead to high price volatility.

If the company eventually managed to generate any profits (or to make the idea of generating profits more probable), then I would be able to reconsider my rating.

data.title: Build-A-Bear's Buybacks Will Still Drive EPS Growth
data.description:
Build-A-Bear’s (NYSE:BBW) multiyear rally might not be over yet. In recent years, the custom toy bear store’s share price has soared because it’s been buying back lots of its shares, and it’s also been posting strong results. Its results for the first half of 2025 were very good. While bearish investors now expect its sales to slow down in the second half of 2025 because of its lower full-year guidance, I still think that its buybacks and expanding margins will make up for this factor.

In the second quarter of 2025, Build-A-Bear reported that its revenue was up 11.1% for the quarter, and its income rose by 12.3% as its margins expanded. The company is performing well on both the top and bottom lines right now because of popular products such as Mini Beans. And because Build-A-Bear is buying back stock at the same time, this resulted in very strong diluted EPS growth of 46.9% this quarter. So its EPS growth was a combination of multiple factors.

Additionally, Build-A-Bear is still trading at a reasonable valuation. After the market close on October 17, it had a trailing P/E ratio of 12.47. So even if its sales growth slows down, margin contraction could be limited because buybacks will still produce EPS growth. The company’s diluted share count has dropped from 16.1 million shares in January 2022 to 13.3 million shares now. Here’s why the bears expect Build-A-Bear’s revenue growth to slow down.

Build-A-Bear’s Guidance Calls For Lower Full-Year Revenue Growth

Build-A-Bear’s management provided weaker guidance for the third and fourth quarters of 2025, and that’s why some investors have lost confidence in its stock. Build-A-Bear posted 11.9% revenue growth in the first quarter, so with 11.1% growth in the second quarter, its revenue growth for the first half of 2025 was 11.5%. But while the company raised its guidance in the second-quarter report, it now expects mid-to-high single-digit revenue growth for the full year. If Build-A-Bear delivers 8% full-year revenue growth, for example, its revenue growth in the second half of the year might be in the 4% range.

Build-A-Bear typically has higher sales in the fourth quarter because consumers buy its bears as holiday gifts. So, weak fourth-quarter results would have a larger impact on its financial statements than weak performance earlier in the year. And if Build-A-Bear is now expecting growth in the 4% range for the second half of the year, financial analysts might also build 2026 projections that call for similar growth rates instead of projecting that the company’s revenue will grow 11% next year.

Build-A-Bear’s Buybacks Will Continue

In the second-quarter report, Build-A-Bear also said that it bought back 167,585 shares of stock for $7.3 million during the first half of the year. It also has $80 million authorized for stock buybacks. So, it’s effectively guiding for more buybacks in 2025. The company’s buybacks have continued during the third quarter and are likely to continue during the fourth quarter as well. And Build-A-Bear’s current buyback authorization is enough to last for a few years at its current buyback rate.

Build-A-Bear can also easily afford to buy back more stock. It forecasts full-year 2025 net income of $66 million at the midpoint, so it has enough income to both buy back stock and pay dividends. It spent $13 million combined on buybacks and dividends during the first half of 2025, so if it returns another $13 million to investors this year, it will still have cash available for other projects, such as store remodels. According to SA, Build-A-Bear has no long-term debt, and its TTM free cash flow is $35 million.

The Main Risk Is That New Tariffs Could Impact Margins

One of the main risk factors for Build-A-Bear is tariffs. It has been buying the parts for its bears from China, and the US government is now engaged in ongoing trade disputes with China. Like other stores, Build-A-Bear reduced the impact of tariffs this year by stockpiling inventory and buying supplies from Southeast Asian countries instead. As the DW article explains, Chinese manufacturers have also been exporting their products from Southeast Asian countries such as Vietnam and Malaysia to avoid high tariffs on Chinese exports.

On the 2Q 2025 earnings call, Build-A-Bear CFO Voin Todorovic went into more detail about Build-A-Bear’s tariff strategy. The CFO explained that Build-A-Bear is now sourcing inventory from Vietnam as well as China. And while the US government has also raised tariffs on Vietnam, the company’s guidance takes the current tariffs on both China and Vietnam into account. So, the main tariff risk for Build-A-Bear is that the US government could add more tariffs that aren’t included in its current guidance in the future.

Build-A-Bear has also increased its prices to mitigate the impact of tariffs. Analysts asked the CFO about the price increases on the call. He explained that Build-A-Bear still achieved 3% traffic growth for the quarter. But the company has recently introduced another product that could help it in that area.

Mini Beans Help Build-A-Bear Maintain Its Margins

Another factor that’s helped the company maintain its pricing is newer product lines such as its Mini Beans, which were introduced in February 2024, according to CEO Sharon Price John. The company often releases new collections of Mini Beans and promotes them as collectors’ items. Mini Beans are small bears with a starting price point of $10 each, although some of them have higher price tags. They’re similar to Beanie Babies, which were once very popular.

Beanie Babies were plush toys that became collectors’ items in the 1990s. Limited edition and rare Beanie Babies were hard to obtain, and collectors were willing to pay high prices for them, so traders began buying Beanie Babies from third parties and reselling them. As a result, some Beanie Babies became very valuable, and they were in high demand in general while the fad was ongoing. It’s not clear if a boom like that will happen with Mini Beans, but they’re also appealing for other reasons.

Build-A-Bear’s prices vary because it sells custom products, but in October 2025, the company’s website said that its largest plush bears cost $98-$128 each. So Mini Beans serve as a more affordable, entry-level product than the company’s custom bears. Additionally, Build-A-Bear is entering into partnerships with third-party toy stores to sell Mini Beans as well. And it has partnered with Sanrio, the owner of Hello Kitty, to create licensed Mini Beans based on Sanrio’s characters.

Comparative Valuation For Build-A-Bear

After the market close on October 17, Build-A-Bear had a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 9.51, which was 9% lower than the median for consumer discretionary businesses. It also had a forward EV/sales ratio of 1.47, which was 11% higher than the median for this sector. So Build-A-Bear doesn’t appear to be trading at a premium valuation, but it doesn’t appear to be trading at a discount either. This is a unique store, so it’s hard to find a direct competitor to use as a comparison, but its valuation seems reasonable in comparison to other consumer discretionary businesses.

Value Estimation For Build-A-Bear

Again, in its second-quarter report, Build-A-Bear called for mid-to-high single-digit revenue growth for the full year of 2025, and the midpoint of its net income guidance is $66 million. Its revenue was $496.4 million in 2024, so if its revenue rose 8% in 2025, it would increase to $536.1 million. Build-A-Bear would have a 12.3% full-year profit margin, up from 10.4% in 2024, for the year.

But now Build-A-Bear is expected to post weaker third- and fourth-quarter results and may post similar results in 2026. If I project that its sales rise by only 4% in 2026, its 2026 revenue would be $579.0 million. To get its 2026 profit margin, I’ll subtract my estimates based on the company’s full-year 2025 guidance from its results for the first half of 2025. Revenue would be $536.1 million - $252.6 million, or $283.5 million, for the second half of 2025. Net income for the second half of 2025 would be $66 million - $27.7 million, or $38.3 million. That’s a 13.5% profit margin for the second half of 2025.

So if Build-A-Bear posted $579.0 million in revenue for 2026, it would generate $78.2 million in net income. At a 2026 P/E of 13, that’s a market cap of $1.017 billion. The company currently has 13.3 million diluted shares outstanding. If I calculate that Build-A-Bear’s diluted share count falls to 13.2 million shares by the end of the year and it buys back 300,000 shares in 2026, its diluted share count would be 12.9 million shares in 2026. This is a conservative estimate considering the company’s past buybacks.

If I divide a market cap of $1.017 billion by 12.9 million diluted shares, I get a 2026 target price of $79. Build-A-Bear’s share price after the market close on October 17 was $56, so my target price is 41% higher than the current share price.

SA analysts and the SA quant score say that Build-A-Bear is a buy right now. Wall Street rates the company a strong buy with a price target of $80, which is 44% higher than the current price. Build-A-Bear has also beaten analysts’ revenue and EPS estimates for the last 5 quarters in a row.

Conclusion

Build-A-Bear’s guidance calls for weaker sales growth during the second half of 2025, but this guidance also implies that its bottom-line performance will continue to improve. The company’s popular Mini Beans collections are helping it maintain its margins, and its guidance includes the impact of the tariffs that are currently in place. So, Build-A-Bear’s stock buybacks should continue to drive strong EPS growth. This stock is also reasonably priced, so there’s no reason to expect multiple compressions either. And the recent drop in stock price has provided a better entry point. So I say that Build-A-Bear is still a buy.

data.title: Forget The Soft Landing: Buy These 2 Dividend Giants To Cushion The Fall
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving.

The history of the Federal Funds Rate has a rather distinct pattern for the past 25 years:

Of course, every interest rate cycle is a little bit different, but the idea that the Fed Funds rate is going to decline to 3% by mid-2026 and then trend sideways through 2027 is a scenario that isn't consistent with history. I'm sure "this time is different," but I'm still putting on my parachute.

What is my parachute? Buying investments that are interest rate sensitive and are likely to continue paying great dividends even through a recession. I want to hold investments that directly benefit from lower interest rates, which means investments like bonds and preferred stock that provide a fixed-rate return.

I also want to buy investments that have a strong correlation with U.S. Treasuries. Because when interest rates come down, that means U.S. Treasuries are going up in price. Basically, if we look at investments that saw a lot of downward pricing pressure when interest rates were rising, those are likely candidates for benefiting when interest rates come back down.

Today, we are going to look at Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA), an ETF that invests in preferred equity and baby bonds. Then we will turn our attention to Dynex Capital, Inc. (DX), a mortgage REIT that focuses on agency MBS. Agency MBS is a unique asset class that is highly correlated with US Treasuries and is capable of large price gains and increased cash flow when interest rates come down, even if economic conditions turn into a recession.

Let's dive in!

Pick #1: PFFA – Yield 9.5%

One of the things that people say to me is that "you should start your own fund." Well, that's one of those things that I'm not interested in doing because I like the freedom to write and state my opinions without having to worry about all the regulatory hoops and hurdles that come with managing a fund. I'm blessed to be at a point in my life where I get to do things that I enjoy and not do things that I don't.

However, if I did run an ETF, it would look a lot like PFFA. InfraCap, the management team of PFFA, led by Jay Hatfield, is on the same wavelength as we are when it comes to opportunities among preferred stock. PFFA drifts away from the typical ETF that just follows an index and isn't afraid to identify unique opportunities.

Looking at PFFA's top holdings, we see several tickers in its top 10 that we hold. Source.

Energy Transfer LP (ET) 9.25% (ET.PR.I) has been one of our favored preferred investments for many years. We were fans of it back when it was CEQP-, a preferred of Crestwood before ET bought it, and the security was trading at much lower prices. Remember those days? Source.

Before ET bought it, CEQP- shares were a lot cheaper, and we were happy to be buying—so was PFFA. This is the kind of opportunity many funds are going to miss out on.

That's the benefit of having active management.

The second feature that makes PFFA different from its peers is that it uses leverage. Using leverage does increase volatility. When a market crashes, being leveraged means that you will see greater downside. If that risk can be managed, then returns can also be significantly higher.

PFFA's management was tested in 2020 by the COVID-19 pandemic. It passed with flying colors, recovering and providing a superior total return relative to a more traditional non-leveraged ETF like PFF:

We believe that PFFA is likely to continue outperforming, primarily thanks to its manager being willing to identify and invest in opportunities in the preferred equity market. So much of the preferred equity market is focused on the banking and insurance sectors. These are sometimes good opportunities, but not always. There are often better opportunities in REITs, MLPs, utilities, and other preferred equity issuers.

Pick #2: DX – Yield 15.2%

Agency MBS (Mortgage-Backed Securities)—a unique asset class that tends to correlate strongly with U.S. Treasuries. Agency MBS was created in an effort to make it easier for the average American to take out a mortgage and buy a home. The "agencies" buy up mortgages that fit their underwriting qualifications, repackage them as MBS, and then guarantee them so that if a borrower defaults, the mortgage will be bought back at par from the investor. This removes the credit risk for the investor, and naturally, that leads to investors who are willing to put a lot more capital into mortgages.

This structure helps ensure that mortgage originators have a buyer for however many mortgages they can originate. They don't have to worry about whether they will have enough capital available at closing to fund the loan.

For investors, it creates an asset class that has minimal credit risk and is an alternative to U.S. Treasuries and has slightly different supply/demand dynamics.

Dynex Capital, Inc. (DX) is a mortgage REIT that focuses on opportunities in agency MBS. DX's primary strategy is to buy agency MBS and leverage it using repurchase agreements, aka "repos."

Repurchase agreements are very cheap financing with rates that are strongly correlated with the Federal Funds rate. During Q3, they paid an average of 4.43% interest. Source.

With the Fed back to cutting, the interest rate they pay will be declining. At the same time, DX has been buying higher-coupon MBS, taking advantage of higher mortgage rates: Source.

While mortgage rates will inevitably come down if the Fed keeps cutting, not all borrowers will rush out and refinance their mortgages. It can create conditions where agency mREITs see a big increase in book value as their portfolio goes from being priced at a discount to par to being priced at a premium. Additionally, cash flow will continue to increase.

Unlike many peers, DX actually let their cash flow turn negative when the yield curve was inverted. They were content to realize gains on their hedges rather than rely on cash flow. Their prior realizations of gains are contributing about $25 million/quarter to their taxable income, while their net interest income has turned positive and has grown from almost nothing to $44.9 million in Q3 2025: Source.

We expect that DX's net interest income will continue to grow, and Q4 will be even better, thanks to the Fed resuming its cutting cycle.

We are in an interesting time for agency mREITs because historically, once rates started declining, they dropped very fast. With the Fed's stutter step this cutting cycle, we've seen these companies have an extended opportunity to issue equity and buy more MBS.

We can see that DX has really ramped up its share issuance over the past two years:

This is a positive because issuing equity is how REITs grow. They have been issuing new equity and buying up agency MBS at a point where agency MBS prices have been low. Source.

If someone is managing a portfolio for us, isn't buying low what we want them to be doing?

We've been buying agency MBS with AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) and Annaly Capital (NLY). DX is a great addition to take advantage of the current low prices and favorable outlook for agency MBS. DX is a much smaller company, and that allows management to make bolder moves like choosing not to use interest rate swaps when the yield curve was inverted. DX's decisions could lead to outperformance relative to peers but also carry the risk of underperformance. Management did a great job through the 2020 COVID crisis, so for those looking for something with a higher risk/reward profile in the agency MBS sector, add DX into the mix.

Conclusion

The Fed resuming its rate-cutting cycle is evidence that the economy is changing, and that typically means that the market is going to change as well. Investors who are basing their picks on what's done well the past 3 years, when interest rates were rising to multi-decade highs, might not be happy with the results if we see the same pattern that's been occurring the past 25 years.

History suggests that when rates start coming down, there comes a point where they are cut quickly and much further than anyone expects. Every rate-cutting cycle, I've seen people pontificate on how "this time is different" and that rates are going to just float down to the "neutral" rate and be flat—we'll have a "soft landing" and there will be unicorns and rainbows, and we're all going to be rich.

Sure it is.

For those of us who build large income streams, the landing might not be soft, but at least we'll have a nice big pile of cash flow to cushion the fall! It's time to make sure you have holdings like PFFA and DX that will provide you with ample dividends as interest rates decline.

data.title: The Great Economic Reawakening - And My 2 Favorite Stocks To Ride It
data.description:
Introduction

One of my more controversial calls this year is that we’re not headed for a recession. I think the exact opposite will happen, as I wrote in articles like the one titled "My Boldest Macro Call Is Unfolding Exactly As I Expected."

In this article, which many called "heavy" and "loaded" because of the density of what I consider very critical developments, I discussed major developments like the high probability of Fed involvement when it comes to reducing long-term rates and improving liquidity:

Here’s an important part of my takeaway back then:

When I say this is one of my most important theses, I am very serious, for a number of reasons. For starters, we need a cyclical recovery and broadening economic growth, as a big part of the economy has been left behind, as dramatic as that may sound. The housing market needs to be unlocked, and manufacturing needs to get a new upswing.

If we add the long-term tailwinds of slow but steady economic reshoring and the data center buildout, I am super bullish on the cyclical economy and believe this could finally give lower-income families who haven’t benefited from the massive stock market surge some support.

[...] the Fed will likely have to apply targeted stimulus to reduce long-term rates without fueling inflation too much. That is very tricky and one of the biggest risks in a "running it hot" economic scenario.

Now, it seems like my thesis is getting more support, in this case from the credit market, which is one of the most important markets, as credit is super cyclical and a market that is much bigger than the equity market.

According to Apollo Global Markets’ Chief Economist Torsten Slok, default rates have peaked and are now moving lower, along with default rates in car loans and credit cards.

This is due to at least three major reasons, which all tell us a lot about the economy in general:

  • Uncertainty related to the trade war is still high, yet lower than it was when President Trump had his Liberation Day press conference. I think this lasts. While we can always expect new trade headlines, I believe recent events have shown the president is not looking to let things escalate again. My personal opinion is that the situation is favorable enough to accelerate long-term economic re-shoring.
  • The AI boom is boosting the buildout of data centers and everything related to this, including infrastructure, power supply, and more. This is one of my favorite parts of the thesis, as it looks like the only thing holding AI innovation back is power and infrastructure. As I see an acceleration in the data center buildout, I think this will be highly beneficial for purchasing managers’ indices like the ISM Manufacturing Index.

  • I also love the third reason, as it perfectly fits my thesis. This reason is that investors are increasingly aware that we are in the early stages of an industrial renaissance in sectors like aerospace, defense, manufacturing, biotech, technology/automation, and others.

That’s why this is the conclusion of Apollo, which I entirely agree with:

In summary, while the trade war remains a mild drag on growth, its impact is being more than offset by the tailwinds from the AI boom and the industrial renaissance. Consequently, there is a growing upside risk that economic growth will reaccelerate over the coming quarters. - Apollo Global Management

Now, let me show you two companies I expect to easily beat the market, supported by two major factors:

  1. A potential recovery in cyclical growth, fueled by both the rapid data center buildout and AI-related efficiencies.
  2. The market isn’t cheap, as we can see below, which means companies with a clear advantage and favorable valuations have an even better chance at beating the market.

So, let’s get to the picks!

#1: Buying Natural Gas Through Range Resources (RRC)

Earlier this week, I wrote an article covering Antero Resources (AR), which is my new trade I executed because I am incredibly bullish on natural gas. I truly think it’s one of the best ways to play the data center buildout, a potential cyclical recovery, exploding LNG exports, and other related factors that could cause a rapid convergence between North American natural gas prices and international prices.

Essentially, my thesis is that after more than a decade (almost two decades) of super-low natural gas prices in North America due to the shale revolution, we’re now seeing a scenario where accelerating exports suck a lot of excess supply out of the market, while booming data center demand further improves the domestic demand picture.

This won’t happen overnight, but create a long-term opportunity for a wide range of companies, as Goehring & Rozencwajg wrote in their latest commentary, who even make the case that supply growth will be a bullish factor:

Look overseas. In Asia, spot LNG trades at $11.50 per mmbtu. In Europe, it’s above $10. Compare that to sub-$3 prices in the U.S. and an almost absurd $0.60 in Canada. Measured by the heat content alone, American gas sells at roughly a 70% discount to world prices; Canadian gas at a 90% discount.

[...] Our own work points to supply losing steam. U.S. gas output, by our estimates, is decelerating rapidly. Against that, add 6 bcf per day of new LNG export capacity scheduled to come online within a year, and the balance tips sharply. Inventories that today feel comfortable could be drawn down faster than expected, and the yawning gap between North American and international prices could begin to close. If it does, it will not be in inches but in leaps. - G&R

In addition to Antero Resources, I really like Range Resources, a major natural gas producer that estimates a shortfall of more than 20 billion cubic feet per day in dry gas production in light of projected 2030 demand.

Just like Antero, Range has everything I’m looking for in a natural gas producer, which includes massive low-cost reserves in my favorite natural gas basin, the Marcellus Basin in the gorgeous Appalachia region, which I still have very high on my travel list, by the way.

In this area, it has more than 30 years of undrilled reserves that are breakeven under $2.50, which gives this company the best reserve profile of all major players in the United States, especially given that nearly 75% of these reserves are breakeven below $2.00.

Moreover, a quarter of its natural gas ends up in the LNG Fairway. More than half of its production is shipped to the Gulf Coast, which has become the demand-growth hotspot.

The main difference to Antero Resources is that Antero Resources has stronger pricing power, as Range Resources usually sells its products at a slight discount to NYMEX Henry Hub, simply because of its marketing strategy. Antero sells more gas to the LNG Fairway and has a bigger footprint in natural gas liquids ("NGL").

The other major difference is hedging. Range has hedged up to 30% of its expected natural gas production and 10% of its NGL output in 2026. That’s roughly twice as much as Antero. Even for 2027, 20% of its production has already been hedged. Most of these hedges have a floor of $4.0 and a ceiling of $4.40 (somewhere in that range, as you can see below).

While Range Resources is still very volatile on the stock market, like all of its peers, it has more conservative management than Antero. They play it safer and are less opportunistic. As I am a bit more aggressive and bullish on natural gas prices, I went with Antero.

However, Range is still a standout. On top of having superior reserves, it also achieved its net debt target of $1.5 billion last year. It now has a mere $1.2 billion in net debt, which is roughly 1.0x EBITDA in the current pricing environment. It also has just $125 million in maturing debt through 2028.

On top of that, it expects to add 400 million cfe in daily production over the next three years with a free cash flow breakeven profile after 2027 at just $2.00 Henry Hub, as long as WTI is at $75, so it can sell its NGLs at $25 per barrel.

Based on a scenario of $3.75 Henry Hub, $75 WTI, and $25 NGLs, it sees a path to more than $1.0 billion in free cash flow by 2027. Because RRC has a market cap of roughly $8.5 billion, this would translate to a FCF yield of 11%.

Even better, at $4.50 Henry Hub, that yield jumps to 13%, as its operating leverage grows the further natural gas prices trade above its breakeven. As I expect Henry Hub to trade at $6-$7 over the next two years, I think we could see a >20% free cash flow yield.

Assuming a 10x free cash flow multiple, which I applied to Antero as well, I think Range Resources has a realistic chance at doubling over the next 12-24 months, with a lot more potential on a longer-term basis due to buybacks, higher output, and a general tailwind from pricing.

Moreover, one major pricing tailwind could be the fact that Germany’s gas reserves are very low compared to usual pre-winter levels. Any cold-weather shock could send international prices in places like Europe through the roof (even if it’s for a short period). It would make Europe compete harder for LNG imports and favor American exports.

RRC started outperforming the market in 2020. That trend peaked in 2022, mainly due to weak commodity prices. Now, I expect the RRC/SPY ratio to bottom, with renewed upside momentum going into the new year.

The next pick is a company I have never discussed before.

#2: Jacobs Solutions (J) Is Another Picks And Shovels Play I Love

I like picks and shovels, which is why I invest in companies like Comfort Systems USA (FIX). I want to profit if I’m right about cyclical growth and data centers. I do not want to be forced to pick which company will be the most successful hyperscaler or which company will have the best chips.

Jacobs Solutions is a company I have never discussed before, which is a shame, as it perfectly fits my thesis. The company is a professional services firm that focuses on a wide range of solutions that all fit major Big Picture ideas of mine, including infrastructure (transportation, water, and urban development), energy projects like grid modernization and nuclear energy support, and digital engineering and defense/government support, among others.

Between now and 2029, the company estimates it can achieve up to 13% annual growth in areas like energy & power and water management, two of the most important areas for the data center buildout. On top of that, transportation, urban planning, and similar operations benefit from AI adoption and a potential cyclical growth recovery.

Honestly, I’m kicking myself for not finding this company sooner.

Because of these tailwinds, it sees between 6% and 8% annual revenue growth through at least 2029. I think the actual results could be close to the upper bound of that range, as it is doing a great job winning new deals, including a deal with NVIDIA (NVDA) to power the next generation of data centers.

Jacobs will test and enhance the end-to-end blueprint workflow, enabling accurate simulations of facility equipment efficiency, throughput and resiliency. Created to unify the design and simulation of billions of components to build digital twins of AI Factories, the blueprint will introduce new integrations across the AI Factory’s power, cooling and network ecosystems. - Jacobs

After having done my homework, I think Jacobs will lead in both data center construction and what I expect to become a boom in advanced manufacturing in the years ahead, when humanoids will become more mainstream, allowing American companies to produce much more efficiently at home without needing low-cost nations. In other words, it will likely accelerate economic re-shoring.

It also aims to return more than 60% of free cash flow to shareholders, free cash flow margins of at least 10%, and net leverage in a very healthy 1.0x to 1.5x range.

Valuation-wise, the J ticker trades at 25.6x earnings. While that may not be cheap compared to its ten-year average of 18.4x, analysts expect an average annual EPS growth rate of 15% over the next four fiscal years.

As I do not expect its valuation multiple to come down due to strong secular growth and potentially recovering cyclical growth, I think we’re looking at more than 14% annual returns.

Although I’m not a fan of covering stocks at an all-time high, I think Jacobs is in a great spot to deliver market-beating returns for many years to come, as growth broadens and the data center buildout becomes more tangible, so to speak.

Takeaway

Slowly but steadily, my thesis of broadening economic growth and improving cyclical demand is gaining steam, supported by three major pillars, two of which rely on AI, including the data center buildout and efficiency gains.

In this article, I covered two companies I expect to beat the market in the years ahead, fueled by these tailwinds and fantastic business models that should have an easy time in a market that is far from cheap.

Range Resources is one of my favorite natural gas producers, which should see massive pricing and volume benefits. Meanwhile, Jacobs is a new company on my coverage list, as it has major exposure in industrial markets, including water, data centers, aerospace, and general modernization.

The only reason I do not own either of these picks is that I already have natural gas exposure through Antero Resources, simply lack the funds right now to add more data center-focused exposure.

Risks To My Thesis

Both companies come with cyclical risks, as rising recession probabilities could hurt natural gas demand and sentiment in areas where Jacobs operates. On top of that, natural gas is ultra volatile, which makes RRC (and most of its peers) the wrong choice for more conservative investors who may be better off buying diversified energy companies like Exxon Mobil (XOM), to name one of the biggest players.

Moreover, negative AI headlines that could hint at a slower-than-expected buildout could hurt both companies in the short term.

data.title: CGI: U.S. Government Shutdown Has Created A Buying Window (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Please note that all $ figures are in $CAD, not $USD, unless otherwise stated.

Introduction

In my last article on CGI Inc. (NYSE:GIB) (TSX:GIB.A:CA) posted over a year ago, I was pretty cautious on the stock following its introduction of a small dividend and strong metrics. Since that time, there have been recent developments (most principally a much more attractive valuation) that have changed my views and led me to upgrade my rating to a ‘buy.’ The latest quarter has shown encouraging revenue growth, strong bookings, and promising progress on M&A integration that point to a more compelling risk-reward profile. While near-term challenges persist from U.S. federal exposure and European market softness, I think the company's conservative balance sheet, expanding international footprint, and disciplined capital allocation give me confidence that CGI is poised for a recovery and potential upside. This article outlines why I believe the current valuation discount is attractive to long-term investors and why now is the time to consider adding the stock to your portfolio.

Q3 '25 Results Were Strong

CGI Inc. reported its Q3 ’25 results on July 30 with revenues of $4.1 billion, reflecting an 11.4% year-on-year increase and 7% growth on a constant currency basis thanks to acquisition contribution and continued momentum in financial services. On the top line, CGI reported a beat of $81.2 million, and on EPS, it showed a 2% beat with EPS coming in at $2.10.

When looking at growth by region, the company’s largest segment, its North American operations, climbed 7.4%, while the European and APAC segments grew a bit slower at 6.6% and 6.4%, respectively. Financial services led industry performance at 9.6% constant currency growth, followed by Government at 8.7%, though softness persisted in Continental Europe’s MRD sector. IP revenue accounted for 20.6% of total revenue, impacted by acquisition dilution and lower U.S. federal IP-enabled business process services volumes.

On the margin front, EBIT margins fell 10bps to 16.3% (as a result of ongoing merger integrations), with overall EBIT clocking in at $666 million. When deducting interest expenses and acquisition-related costs, EBT came out to $552 million for a 13.5% margin. Given the strength of the cash generation here, I’d say that the results were consistent, albeit the margins were unimpressive. With respect to capital allocation, the company spent $105 million in business investments, $286 million in share buybacks, and $34 million in dividends.

Regarding the balance sheet, CGI maintains a conservative financial position with low levels of leverage. With a little over $4 billion of long-term debt spread out across maturities, the leverage ratios are conservative, with a net debt to capitalization ratio of 23.4%. Rated A3 by Moody's and BBB+ by S&P, both with a stable outlook, the credit profile is stable, and the strong ratings indicate that CGI should have no problem tapping debt capital markets when it comes time to refinance existing debt or take on additional leverage.

In terms of some of the key highlights from the quarter, CGI had total bookings exceeding $4 billion for a 101% book-to-bill ratio, led by growth from both commercial and state governments (U.S.) at a 121% ratio, Finland, Poland, and the Baltics at a 113% ratio, and Scandinavia, Northwest, and Central East Europe at 106%. Notable contract wins this quarter included a $200 million extension with the State of California for end-to-end managed services, the European Space Agency’s climate monitoring initiative, Finnish customs modernization, NHS Scotland’s user-centric digital platform, Texas Comptroller’s office financial system unification, and the FAA’s modernized Notice to Airmen system. With some big government and blue chip names added to the order book, it looks to be business as usual for CGI despite the share price heading lower.

What To Watch In Q4 '25

Looking towards Q4’25, I think investors should be watching the company’s U.S. federal bookings, as the ongoing government shutdown, now in its third week, risks derailing the early stabilization management flagged with that sequential $250 million uptick. The shutdown's freeze on non-essential discretionary spending and procurement, as well as furloughs potentially delaying IT contract awards, could squeeze CGI's IP-enabled BPO volumes further. This would be especially negative if we were to see this drag past mid-November amid stalled ACA subsidy talks. I feel this adds urgency to monitoring backlog conversion rates, and any slippage below the 2x revenue coverage might signal deeper federal headwinds, though essential services should keep some cash flowing. On the flip side, tariff negotiations with the EU and Japan are yielding phased relief (capping auto tariffs at 15% effective October 14), which could unlock pent-up manufacturing spend in Continental Europe, where MRD softness has lingered. My sense is that if these deals firm up by quarter-end, they might nudge organic growth off the floor management described. That said, this prolonged uncertainty could keep CGI’s clients in wait-and-see mode.

My outlook for Q4 is that merger integration metrics will be the real litmus test, with BJSS's full-quarter embed and Apside's pending close potentially helping U.K. and France margins if cross-sell wins materialize as hoped. I believe watching AI pipeline conversion (now up 10% year on year) will reveal how quickly clients shift savings from automation to transformation projects, a trend management tied to renewed financial services momentum. Longer term, the shutdown's $15 billion weekly economic drag underscores volatility and uncertainty, so it’s no surprise to me that the share price is down. The question is, is it overdone?. I’ll discuss more about this in the next section as well as the ‘Valuation’ section.

Why Now Is The Time To Buy

Despite shares treading water at 52-week lows, I think the outlook isn’t all that bad. As history has shown time and time again, CGI has grown its business under both Democrats and Republicans. Moreover, the business has become increasingly diversified geographically, with increased contributions coming from international markets.

As I alluded to in a piece I wrote on Leidos (LDOS) in February of this year, when it seemed like policy uncertainty could put a damper on the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) and a cut to government spending, it’s often wise to go against the grain when headlines hit stocks. In the case of Leidos, the company experienced little impact to contracts, and the shares rebounded. If you bought the stock in February, you’d be sitting on gains of around 35% compared to the S&P 500's return of 12% over the same time period.

While I’m not trying to suggest that the Leidos situation is identical to CGI, I do believe that the market has unfairly reacted to news rather than fairly assessed the impact on CGI. Firstly, most of these government shutdowns tend to be extremely short. While it certainly could take another 30 days for the situation to resolve itself, I’m quite sure that we can be cautiously optimistic about the future trajectory of the overall business. Most likely, projects might be delayed 3-6 months tops, but they will continue.

On the international front, it's certainly a positive that tariff talks with the EU and Japan are progressing with auto tariffs capped at 15%. To me, this could finally ease client caution in manufacturing and help organic growth in Continental Europe start to recover, though it may take a quarter or two to show up.

When looking at street estimates for revenue and EPS growth, the sell-side has mostly taken down their numbers (perhaps rightfully so) with soft bookings, but those estimates impacted not just Q3/Q4 for FY’25 but also for FY’26 (source: Bloomberg). In my view, while better organic growth and more M&A are likely needed in order for the stock to work, I think there’s a good chance that one or both of these catalysts will transpire over the next year or so. In the meantime, I view Street estimates as achievable and see limited room for further multiple compression.

Valuation Is Attractive

Overall, the sell-side has taken down their price targets, but most are still bullish with 12 ‘buy’ ratings, 1 ‘hold’ rating, and 2 ‘sell’ ratings (source: Bloomberg). With an average price target of $158.41, analysts see about 29.5% upside over the next twelve months.

While that implied upside might look aggressive, I don’t believe it to be unrealistic. . Why? When looking at the valuation, CGI’s multiple has come in considerably. Previously, CGI garnered a higher multiple in line with the market, but today, it trades closer to 13.6x earnings and 8.9x EV/EBITDA (source: Bloomberg).

With potential catalysts on the horizon like Q4/25 results (to be reported next month in November), potential M&A, a re-acceleration in organic revenue growth, and a potential for a cyclical recovery in SI&C revenue, there are many reasons to still be bullish. I think a discount to CGI’s historical average multiple of 17x is warranted, but not this steep.

Takeaway

In summary, CGI’s third quarter showed a business model that could face some more near-term headwinds when we look to Q4 and into 2026 despite double-digit revenue growth and strong bookings and backlog. No question about it; investors are concerned about the future. While the U.S. government shutdown and lingering MRD softness in Continental Europe introduce uncertainty for Q4 and beyond, the company's conservative balance sheet, diversified geographic footprint, and progressing tariff relief with the EU and Japan position it well for recovery. Trading at compressed multiples, my view is that the current share price selloff is likely overdone. With conservative sell-side estimates, the ingredients are there for CGI to beat in the quarters ahead. As such, I’m upgrading my rating from a ‘hold’ to a ‘buy’ on CGI.

data.title: FDL: Dividend Income And Capital Preservation When It Counts
data.description:
Building a portfolio of dividend-paying companies can have many goals, and I believe that the First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders Index Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:FDL) can be a relevant investment if you are an income investor looking for capital preservation. At first glance, looking at the risk-adjusted performance will make you think that FDL is not a good fund, as it is more volatile than the market for less total returns, but a closer look will reveal that the fund is very efficient at mitigating drawdown risk and providing you with a risk profile uncorrelated with that of the market.

FDL is relevant as a defensive fund; the risk-adjusted underperformance is still an attention point, and I wouldn’t recommend it as a core holding, but if you are looking for risk diversification and income, you should pay attention to this fund. With this purpose in mind, I give it a buy rating.

FDL's High-Yield, High-Concentration Strategy

The First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders Index Fund is a passive ETF replicating the performance of the Morningstar Dividend Leaders Index. This index is itself based on the highest yielding companies in the Morningstar Dividend Composite Index, itself selecting companies that have a record of consistent dividend payments and the capacity to sustain their dividends in the Morningstar US Market Index. Overall, the investment universe is very close to the Russell 1000, so I will use it as a reference for this analysis (all the companies of FDL are listed in the Russell 1000). The fund is selecting the 100 companies with the highest dividend yield and is weighting them by the indicated dividend yield. This creates a portfolio of companies with a strong track record of paying and increasing dividends, but it also means that it is going to be very concentrated.

To assess how different the approach of FDL is as opposed to the market, I will use the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB) as a reference, as it is the largest ETF tracking the Russell 1000 index, with a traditional market-cap approach and a portfolio of 1011 companies.

As FDL comes with many screening layers, its portfolio turnover is high at 46%, meaning that, on average, a company in the portfolio is going to stay two years. Considering that to be included in the fund, a long track record is needed, I find this to be quite short. The portfolio is rotating fast, and this is not because of the market, as the Russell 1000 is very stable with a portfolio turnover of 3%. For FDL, this implies higher costs, and will mechanically hurt the income flows.

A Defensive Value Tilt

The portfolio's strategic positioning is inherently defensive because of the focus on dividend growth and level. This also implies a strong value positioning, as you can see from the fund’s valuation metrics. FDL’s P/E ratio is 13.81, and its P/B ratio is 1.84 against 28.98 and 5.09 for IWB, our market benchmark. This is a significant difference that indeed is showing us that FDL is not looking for growth.

This is also clear from looking at the portfolio, I do find it a bit upsetting to see how concentrated it is, however, even if history has shown us that this was a relevant positioning. The fund’s top 10 holdings account for more than half of the portfolio (56.56%), and the top 3 alone account for more than a quarter of the portfolio (25.84%). You can see the key holdings below:

The concentration of the fund is massive and contrasts a bit with its potential as a capital preservation vehicle, when the market benchmark is showing us a top 10 concentration of 35.62%. But, as you can see, all of the portfolio companies are value ones, with strong cash generation, and this focus is also clear when looking at the sectoral split:

The value tilt is evident with a focus on stable sectors with stable cash flows and dividends, such as Energy, Health Care, Consumer Staples, and Financials. This is a clear contrast with the market benchmark, as the fund avoids the growth tech sector. We could say that FDL is an “old economy” fund with a focus on oil giants, pharmaceutical firms, and consumer brands, while the market is favoring growth companies like tech and communication giants. This is a relevant positioning to diversify your risk exposure.

The Trade-Off: Performance For Protection

Looking at the total returns of FDL is clearly showing that the fund is underperforming the market.

You can see from the tables above that FDL’s positioning really lags the market over the last 3 years amid the AI stocks surge, but on longer time frames, the difference is not as wide (while still underperforming). However, the reason why FDL is interesting is not its performance; it is its risk management potential, which is not evident at first glance.

If you look at risk-adjusted metrics with Sharpe ratios, you will first think that I am mistaken. For FDL, we have 0.69 (3 years), 0.77 (5 years), and 0.62 (10 years) against 1.32 (3 years), 0.80 (5 years), and 0.83 (10 years) for IWB: strictly inferior. This is coming from the lower performance, but also from the volatility measured with the standard deviation, not a strong case for FDL: 15.40% (3 years), 16.10% (5 years), and 15.72% (10 years) against 13.68% (3 years), 16.13% (5 years), and 15.62% (10 years). Why am I saying that FDL is a good fund, then?

The answer is that the standard deviation is an incomplete measure, and quite frankly, for a long-term investor (that you probably are if you pay attention to income strategies), it only has a limited interest. What I like about FDL is its potential for capital preservation when the market is going down. Over the last 5 years, the maximum drawdown of IWB was -24.67% in January 2022 amid interest rate fears and an uncertain outlook for growth companies that were at the center (and that still are) of market dynamics. Now, if we look at FDL’s maximum drawdown, we will see something unique. The fund’s maximum drawdown is only -15.66%, and it didn’t happen in 2022.

The maximum drawdown of the fund actually occurred in February 2023 because of two signals: the “higher‑for‑longer” interest‑rate projection and the softer energy pricing that significantly affected the fund. Two effects cumulated, and this resulted in a drawdown of only -15.66%. This is the strength of FDL: its resilience during market turmoil, and its continued income stream during that time, which we will mention in the next part.

Other Financial Metrics And Outlook

FDL is an income fund, so the core of its appeal is its dividend distribution. The fund is providing investors with a dividend yield of 4.05% backed by a 4.29% 30-day SEC yield: highly resilient. This is a 3.05 percentage point difference with the 1.00% dividend yield of IWB. The dividend yield of the fund is resilient, but oscillating between 3% and 4.5% historically, as you can see from the chart below:

However, the higher dividend yield and multi-layer strategy come with a high expense ratio of 0.43%, which might be too high for some investors. This is a 0.28 percentage point premium to pay over IWB’s 0.15%, one additional reason not to consider FDL as a core holding but rather as a complement for risk management.

In terms of size, FDL is a popular fund with $5.8 Bn in AUM, a significant amount when you consider that the larger Russell 1000 fund is “only” at $43.9 Bn. The average daily trading volume of the fund is $39.7 Mn (0.69% of the fund), enough to ensure a comfortable median bid/ask spread of 0.02%.

Overall, I think that the positioning of the fund is making sense in the current market environment, as value and defensive assets could be favored again. In its "Heliocentrism" paper, JP Morgan highlights that the energy sector could be a very beneficial investment, as the decarbonization trend will take years to fully happen and the need for traditional energy sources is still very high, while the factor is not accordingly priced. This means that leading mature companies with stable cash flows, prioritizing shareholder returns, could see a strong regain of favor in the coming months.

The consumer staples sector is “returning to normal” and will prove once more that it is a sector that can show a strong performance when the broad economic growth fails to meet high expectations, which is precisely what we could see now. The health care sector has seen difficult times recently with short- to medium-term headwinds, but this means that the sector is now priced poorly and that it could be a relevant entry point with a value focus, that FDL is providing you. The long-term defensive characteristics are still here.

The valuations of the broad U.S. equity market are "stretched" and the risk for investors with too strong an exposure to market-cap ETFs is that a rotation to undervalued sectors in response to the large valuation of mega-cap growth stocks could hurt market returns while favoring value investments. This is what FDL can provide you with.

Risks

Concentration Risk: Despite its defensive profile and capital preservation advantage, the significant concentration of the fund, with 56% of the fund invested in the top 10, doesn’t make sense. This leaves the portfolio very exposed to industry-specific downturns affecting the top 10 companies, like a drop in oil prices or a single company bad-news like one about Exxon Mobil (XOM). Without this concentration, FDL would have been a much better fund.

Interest Rate Sensitivity: The high-dividend portfolio can act like a bond proxy, meaning that stocks in sectors such as Utilities, Staples, and Telecom sectors will react in a similar way. This is a good thing if interest rates are going down, which is what the market is anticipating, but some underlying risk exists because of the stickier-than-expected inflation.

Persistent Underperformance: As FDL is more of a risk mitigation ETF, it comes with a strong opportunity cost, especially in a growth driven market. For now, holding FDL means getting lower returns than you would have with a traditional market replicating ETF, and you must be confident in your need for defensive strategies to go with FDL.

Value Trap Risk: The strategy of the fund to select high yield companies with only looking at a few years of track record can create a portfolio made of value trap companies. This is a significant risk given the lack of other proper screening in the construction of the portfolio, and given the high expense ratio, I think this is a real miss.

Conclusion

The First Trust Morningstar Dividend Leaders Index Fund is an interesting fund to look at if you are in the market for income ETFs with a strong capital preservation potential. I wouldn’t advise it as a core holding or as your only capital preservation ETF because of the risks I just mentioned above, but for diversification purposes, I think it is a very solid ETF to own, especially in the current macroeconomic environment, where getting defensive investments of high quality is becoming increasingly important.

data.title: Healthcare Realty Trust: Shaky Dividend During Strategic Overhaul
data.description:
Healthcare REITs are the top-performing REIT sector of 2025, returning an average of 22.9%, outperforming the REIT average of 5.43%, the Nasdaq 100 at 18.6%, and the S&P 500 at 14.4%.

Healthcare Realty Trust (NYSE:HR), however, has lagged the sector year-to-date, posting a share price gain of 10.94% while yielding 6.50% on cost, for a total return of 17.44%. That does beat the S&P 500, however.

Meet the Company

Healthcare Realty Trust was founded in 1992 and is headquartered in Nashville. As of June 30, the company owned 619 MOBs (medical office buildings) totaling 36.1 msf (million square feet). The vast majority (85%) are multi-tenant facilities, and nearly three-quarters (73%) are located adjacent to or on the campus of a hospital. The company enjoys approximately 89% occupancy and expects that figure to rise to 90% by year's end.

The largest geographical concentrations are in Houston and Dallas, and of the top 10 markets, eight are in the Sunbelt.

These 10 markets comprise 46.2% of the company's total portfolio, which spans 35 U.S. states.

HR bought out Healthcare Trust of America in July 2022 and has been in the process of disposing of low-performing properties ever since. The company has completed about $900 million in asset sales year-to-date.

Due to what company executives call "consistent underperformance," as of July 2025, HR is undergoing a complete overhaul of all facets of the organization, as shown in the chart below.

This is an ambitious and sweeping overhaul, and the jury remains out on how well management can pull it off. The plan involves the disposition of 12% of the company's assets, which will take a toll on operating revenues. It also involves reducing the dividend in order to reinvest in the portfolio.

I wrote about this company last October. At that time, I rated shares a Sell based on a heavy debt load, a shaky dividend, and negative revisions. Sure enough, shares sold off from $17.56 on the day the article was published, drifting down to $14.12 on June 3, according to MarketWatch. That was a plunge of (-19.6)% over a period of nearly nine months. Since that date, however, share prices have gained altitude to $18.83. So price performance over the year-and-change since publication stands at just +7.23%. Coupled with the yield on cost of 6.66% over that period, the total return has been a very decent +13.9%.

Let's take a look at where the company's metrics stand now and try to divine its likely path.

Growth Metrics

Here are the three-year growth figures for FFO (funds from operations) and EFCO (earnings from continuing operations).

Metric 2022 2023 2024 2025 3-year CAGR
FFO (millions) $297 $547 $193 $427 --
FFO Growth % -- 84.2 (-64.7) 121.2 12.86%
FFO per share 1.75 1.69 1.56 $1.59** $1.59
FFO per share growth % -- (-3.4) (-7.7) 1.9 (-3.15)%
EFCO (millions) $41 (-$282) (-$664) (-$408) --
EFCO Growth % -- NA NA NA NA

Source: Seeking Alpha Premium, iREIT+Hoya Capital, and author calculations

* Trailing 12 months

** Consensus estimate, based in part on company guidance.

This is by far the wildest and most volatile revenue picture I have ever seen in a REIT. FFO surges by high double digits one year, such as 2023 and 2025, then plummets the next, such as 2024. The 12.86% three-year CAGR for FFO is mostly an artifact of the merger. On a per-share basis, FFO is more consistent and trending downward (-3.15)% per annum. Meanwhile, cash flow is a mess, with the company hemorrhaging over a billion dollars in EFCO in the past two years.

Meanwhile, here is how the stock price has done over the past three twelve-month periods, compared to the REIT average as represented by the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ).

Metric 2022 2023 2024 2025 3-yr CAGR
HR share price Oct. 20 $18.74 $14.53 $18.22 $18.88 --
HR share price Gain % -- (-22.5) 25.4 3.6 (0.25)
VNQ share price Oct. 20 $77.54 $72.40 $97.51 $92.09 --
VNQ share price Gain % -- (-6.7) 34.7 (-5.6) 5.90

Source: MarketWatch.com and author calculations

While shares in the average REIT have gained an average of 5.90% per annum, shares of HR have traded essentially flat (0.25%). So investors in HR have gained almost exactly the yield on cost from the dividend, and nothing more. Since HR's yield has tended to be higher than the yield on the average REIT, this underperformance is ameliorated somewhat.

Balance Sheet Metrics

HR has a bond-rated balance sheet, but the company's debt ratio and debt/EBITDA, while not terrible, are considerably worse than the overall REIT average and the sector average. The company is carrying no variable rate debt.

Company Debt Ratio Debt/EBITDA Var. Bond
HR 45% 6.7 0.0% BBB

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, Seeking Alpha Premium, and author calculations

The company is carrying $4.9 billion in long-term debt. Of that amount, $629 million is due next year, and a daunting $1.15 billion comes due in 2027. Thereafter, another $800 million comes due over the next two years. Interest coverage is a narrow 0.20, as shown below in the discussion of the dividend.

Dividend Metrics

At first blush, HR's dividend yield looks good at 5.11%. However, in light of the company's track record for cutting the dividend and its strategic plan to continue cutting it in order to reinvest in the portfolio, its yield ends up looking average to less than average.

Company Div. Yield 3-yr Div. Growth Div. Score Payout Div. Safety
HR 5.11% (-8.18)% 3.96 60% F

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, Seeking Alpha Premium, and author calculations

Dividend Score projects the yield three years from now on shares bought today, assuming the dividend growth rate remains unchanged.

The Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings system rates the company's dividend as unsafe, at an F, implying a 40% chance of another cut in the coming 12 months. HR recently reduced its dividend from $0.31 to $0.24, beginning with the most recent payout.

The table below sheds light on why the dividend is considered so unsafe. Interest coverage is poor, and the various payout ratios are still very high, even after the recent cut.

Valuation Metrics

HR appears to be attractively priced, at 11.8x FFO '25. However, most likely it is cheap for a reason, and research shows that cheap REITs tend to stay cheap. Judged by NAV, HR is selling at a slight premium of 3.2%, while the average REIT sells at a slight discount of (-2.8%). The company's three-year track record for FFO growth per share is sub-zero.

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital

What Could Go Right?

If the company's strategic overhaul is a success and comes in ahead of schedule, that could change my thesis. If the company manages to boost its FFO per share to the point where the payout ratios become reasonable and the dividend becomes safe, that could also change my thesis.

Earnings Preview

HR reports Q3 earnings on Friday morning, Oct. 31.

The table below summarizes last quarter's performance and the consensus expectations for this quarter.
