As you can see, in Q2, HR managed a slight beat of 2 cents on normalized FFO but missed on EPS on a GAAP basis by a comparatively wide margin, as analysts were expecting a loss of only 8 cents per share, compared to the 45-cent loss the company reported. Total revenue, on the other hand, beat by 1.4%, coming in $4.29 million higher than expected.

Analysts are now expecting the reverse this quarter. They expect:

  • A slight decline of (-2.4)% in FFO to $0.40, right in line with the $1.59 expected for the year,
  • An improvement almost to the break-even point in EPS, and
  • A sag of (-2.4%) in revenue.

Although none of these move the needle much in evaluating the company's prospects going forward, at the same time none of it inspires confidence in me as an investor that the company will be in a better position to preserve its dividend in the near future.

Investor's Bottom Line

A 40% chance of a dividend cut is too much risk for my money. If the dividend is cut, a sell-off in shares is likely to follow, leaving the investor with the worst of both worlds. So in the short term, HR is a Sell, to my way of thinking. Two to five years down the road, shares bought today could do OK, especially for investors who are only interested in the dividend yield, but even for such investors, it would be better to wait for a more favorable yield at purchase. The current yield of 5.11% is only about 100 to 125 basis points above the no-risk rate on Treasuries, and that's a pretty thin equity risk premium.

The Seeking Alpha Quant system rates HR a Hold, as does Zacks. CFRA and Argus both rate the company a Sell.

data.title: Constellation Energy: Long-Term Powerhouse, Wait For Potential Correction And Upcoming Opportunity
data.description:
Constellation Energy Corporation (NASDAQ:CEG) is the largest producer of carbon-free electricity in the United States, with a core focus on nuclear power generation. The company operates a fleet of zero-emission nuclear, hydro, wind, and solar assets, providing reliable baseload and renewable power to utilities, large commercial customers, and government institutions. They are particularly strong in nuclear, and have a fleet large enough to power over 16 million homes in the U.S.

I gave CEG a buy rating in March, and the stock has returned 71% since that time. I really do believe in CEG long-term, but at the moment, I have the stock as a hold. If you own, I don’t believe you should sell, but for those on the sidelines, I think waiting a few months could be beneficial. CEG has doubled in valuation since I last wrote on the stock, and in earnings, since then, it has shown some weakening in operations and profitability.

On the other hand, they are still a powerhouse in the nuclear field, which is in a great long-term position due to spiking demand from data centers, and backing from the current administration. They have strength in their generation capacity and fleet, and are on the brink of officially beginning a key merger at the end of this year.

The stock is definitely worth a hold, I just suspect there could be a bit of a correction until a forward switch in margins, and the synergy of their merger.

Dragging Margins

Margins for CEG have been under pressure in 2025 and have fallen from peaks seen in late 2024. Net income margin is right in line with the sector median at 12%, with gross profit margin at 21.77%, less than half of the sector average.

Profitability is not going to rise when expenses are outpacing its sales. Operating expenses are up 18% in the first half of 2025 versus a year ago, with operating revenues up only 11% in that time. Purchased power and fuel costs are the leading cause, up 32% YoY based on the first six months of the year. Management referred to this from higher energy prices and a larger customer base, specifically in the Mid-Atlantic and ERCOT regions.

As you can see, operating income is down largely from a year ago. Net income in the first half of 2024 was $1.647 billion and has dropped to $957 million in the first half of 2025. With a growing service base leading to greater expenses, I would like to see sales grow at a quicker pace and margins get back on track.

Valuation

Current P/E for the stock is just shy of 39x, which is close to double the sector median and also over double from when I last wrote on CEG in the spring. Price to book is 4x its industry peer’s median, and PEG is right at the average.

Valuation is the biggest element as to why I don’t still have the stock as a buy. With growth having slowed a bit, but the share price still climbing, I see a correction as likely in the coming months.

However, CEG had a P/E near 40x in April of 2024, and now the valuation is back to the same, with a doubling in the share price. I could be wrong, but I think based on a few years of history, a fairer valuation for CEG is closer to 30x, especially based on the lighter YoY revenue growth thus far in 2025.

Upcoming Opportunities

In January, CEG sealed a deal to acquire Calpine with an equity purchase price of $16.4 billion. The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission approved the transaction in July, and it is expected to be finalized in Q4 this year.

CEG already has about 22.8 GW of nuclear generating capacity. This is more than double the second-place nuclear fleet of Duke Energy (DUK), that holds 11.2 GW capacity. CEG has an additional 10 GW capacity through natural gas, oil, wind, and solar.

Calpine is particularly strong in natural gas and geothermal, operating across 79 plants in the United States. They have 26 GW of natural gas capacity with 425 MW under construction. They also hold 13 geothermal plants, bolstering business for CEG in areas they have not necessarily excelled in. The addition is anticipated to be EPS accretive by over 20% in 2026, and to add over $2 billion in free cash flow annually.

I think earnings for the remainder of 2025 could be subdued, but once the Calpine addition synergizes, the earnings boost that comes with it should help lower their raised valuation. I expect this acquisition to be a strong strategic move and think that by Q2 2026, the numbers are going to show.

Another upcoming project for CEG is the restart of the Three Mile Island Unit 1 nuclear plant in Pennsylvania for Microsoft (MSFT). The now named Crane Clean Energy Center is ahead of schedule, is 80% staffed, and looks to be on track for reopening in 2027. A 20-year agreement is in place for CEG to provide clean, carbon-free electricity for MSFT data centers in arguably the world’s most explosive industry right now. This long-term deal with one of the world’s largest companies and leaders in AI and cloud growth, is a massive win and shows trust that other huge players could look to soon.

Conclusion

Existing data center customers is a growing space for CEG. This is the industry to be involved in right now, as growth is absolutely exploding with giant caps investing heavily. CEG is in a great position to ride the data center wave well into the future, as a nuclear capacity leader, and grow through other energy sources by acquisition.

The Trump administration is another favorable piece to the puzzle for CEG, too. The President has made it clear he is “pro-nuclear” and has made life more challenging for other clean energy sources. Recently, Trump has advocated to halt offshore wind expansion and has removed several tax advantages to the solar industry.

I really do like CEG as a long play, but for those not in on the fun yet, I rate CEG a hold, as I think a correction could be forthcoming into early 2026. Valuation has doubled in about six months, and expenses have risen quicker than revenue this year. I do think that will turn around, in early-mid 2026. Hold CEG for now and check back in at the new year.

data.title: Prologis: Data Centers Could Meaningfully Accelerate Growth
data.description:
I would rather prep for a colonoscopy than issue Berkshire shares. - Warren Buffett

Prologis (NYSE:PLD) has been one of the best REITs at compounding value, in large part thanks to its mostly self-funded model, where the company rarely issues shares. Instead, it develops assets and then recycles capital once they have been stabilized. While the company has issued a significant number of shares in its history, it has been mostly to pay for acquisitions, and not as a regular funding mechanism.

In any case, the results speak for themselves, with Prologis having delivered a Core Funds From Operations (FFO) per share compounded annual growth rate (CAGR) of 12% over the past five years, enabling the dividend for its part to grow at a 13% CAGR. Still, total return performance over the last couple of years has been disappointing, but it appears many of the headwinds are starting to dissipate. After excessive building of logistics assets in the post-pandemic period, deliveries, and in particular new construction, are now meaningfully below average. This is creating a much more favorable supply backdrop for the company. In addition, there remains compelling embedded upside as the company renews leases, given that in-place rent is significantly below market rents. Finally, the company is likely to see growth boosted by data center opportunities.

Q3 Financial Results

Prologis reported solid Core FFO of $1.49 per share for the third quarter, ahead of analyst estimates, and also increased full-year guidance. Perhaps more importantly, there was an inflection in cash Same Store Net Operating Income (NOI) after seeing several quarters of deceleration.

Similarly, there was an inflection in total occupancy at the end of the period, the first such increase in many quarters. This was mostly the result of occupancy improvement in North America, as some international markets remain very weak.

At the same time, Prologis is going on the offensive, expanding its already vast land portfolio to prepare for new opportunities, which could increasingly be data centers. As we'll see, the data center could grow to become a meaningful new engine of growth.

Data Center Opportunity

During the most recent earnings call, company President Dan Letter described extremely well why Prologis is well suited to pursue the data center opportunity, including its land bank, availability of power, and thousands of well-located buildings that will be particularly important for AI inference applications, where distance to the user matters even more.

[..] every megawatt we can deliver over the next 3 years is already accounted for in conversations. So we have a big tailwind behind us there. And then if you think about our land bank, our 14,000 acres of land that we own or control, you look at our 6,000 buildings in these infill locations and think about how well we are set up for not only the current wave of AI demand, but the next wave, which will be inference.

In the U.S. there has been a clear inflection in data center demand the past few years, and the opportunity is now valued in the trillions of dollars. During the quarter, Prologis moved an additional 1.5 GW of data center capacity to its advanced stage, increasing the total to approximately 5.2 GW. In dollar terms, the company estimates this would amount to close to $15 billion in investment as a powered shell, and possibly four times as much if they were to be delivered as turnkey solutions.

To understand why Prologis is putting so much focus on the data center opportunity, it is crucial to look at the development economics. Whereas a typical warehouse can deliver a value creation margin of 15% to 20%, data centers are currently in the 25% to 50% range. In other words, on average Prologis can generate more than twice the value when building data centers, and the opportunities also allow the company to put more capital to work, as the required investments are about 7x higher.

Balance Sheet

Prologis can afford to pursue these data center opportunities in large part thanks to having one of the most solid balance sheets in the REIT sector (VNQ). It has an enviable 3.2% weighted average interest rate with well-staggered maturities and a long weighted-average duration of more than 8 years. It is therefore not surprising that it is highly rated by both Moody's (MCO) with an 'A2' credit rating, and S&P Global (SPGI) which assigns the company an 'A' investment grade credit rating. With the balance sheet in great shape, and over $7 billion in liquidity, Prologis is very well positioned to finance new opportunities.

Rent Growth

Even as Prologis invests for the future, its core industrial business continues to deliver strong results. Industrial property fundamentals cooled off in 2023 and 2024 after the pandemic induced ecommerce surge. New warehouse supply pushed U.S. vacancy up to roughly 7.5% nationally. However, there are clear signs that the "mini-cycle" downturn is bottoming out. CEO Moghadam said the logistics market is nearing an inflection point for growth, calling it "one of the most compelling setups I’ve seen in 40 years", as rents and occupancy show signs of reaccelerating.

The mark-to-market rent gap opportunity in Prologis' portfolio is enormous, which virtually guarantees growth as leases roll over.

So really, what you and we and everybody else has to handicap is what is the catch-up slope from where we are today to that higher trend line, which is going to grow over time with inflation and all that. But that trend line is significantly above today's rents. We can argue how much -- and I think it's about 40% over in place and probably 25 -- 20% to 25% above market rents today.

Shareholder Returns

Prologis has a strong track record of increasing its dividend, more than doubling the quarterly payout over the past decade. While the future growth rate is likely to be more modest, the current dividend yield is relatively attractive at ~3.2%.

Future Outlook

Embedded rental gains alone provide a multiyear growth runway, and the data center opportunity could further boost growth. Its high-quality, supply-constrained locations form a powerful moat. These are sites where new supply is limited, making them ideal for serving AI data center demand that requires proximity to end-users for low latency and reliable power. The data center business is also complementary to the distributed energy business Prologis has been building, which CFO Timothy Arndt expects solar generation capacity will exceed 1 GW by the end of the year.

[...] our energy business delivered 28 megawatts of solar generation and storage in the quarter. With 825 megawatts of current capacity, we are on track to deliver on our 1-gigawatt goal by year-end. Interest from customers remains robust against the backdrop of increasing energy prices and forecasted shortages in power. We continue to integrate our solar storage and off-grid energy solutions with our real estate, another example of how Prologis continues to evolve with and for our customers.

Given the improved trends in warehouse demand and reduced supply, and improved leasing activity, Prologis increased full-year guidance for Core FFO per share to a range of $5.83 to $5.86, and occupancy of 95% at the midpoint.

Valuation

Prologis is trading with a price/FFO per share somewhat above its historical average. However, considering that it is just exiting a down cycle and that there are massive embedded growth opportunities in the difference between in-place and market rents, not to mention the data center opportunity, we believe the premium is justified. After all, pure-play data center REITs like Digital Realty (DLR) and Equinix (EQIX) trade at meaningfully higher multiples.

Alternatively, looking at the average analyst estimate for net asset value (NAV), shares appear reasonably valued, with potential upside from new business opportunities such as the energy and data center businesses.

Risks

One major risk is operational execution in the new data center business. Building and operating data centers is complex. Incumbents like Digital Realty and Equinix have deep relationships with hyperscalers and enterprise clients.

Prologis will need to prove it can deliver power-dense facilities on time and on budget. Supply chain issues for electrical gear, generators, cooling systems, and other critical components could also cause expensive delays.

Conclusion

If Prologis executes well, the next five years could see accelerating earnings. The combination of a rebounding logistics market, significant embedded rent growth, and high-margin data center projects, could lift FFO growth above its historical mid-single-digit growth rate.

There are reasons to be optimistic the data center business can significantly boost growth. Prologis has the locations, the capital, and now the intent to repurpose and expand into this profitable space. If industrial property fundamentals continue to stabilize as expected, and the AI revolution drives sustained demand for data centers, Prologis stands to benefit on both fronts. Current share prices assume solid performance, but not necessarily a transformative jump to much faster growth if the data center engine gains enough scale.

data.title: New Era Of Growth For U.S. Liquefied Natural Gas Exports
data.description:
By Brian Watson, Senior Portfolio Manager and Casey Wolf, Senior Analyst

Natural gas exports are one of the largest drivers of the increasing demand for liquefied natural gas (LNG). And export demand will most likely continue to grow meaningfully beyond 2030.1 (Artificial intelligence is also an demand important driver.) The US is the largest exporter of LNG in the world. It moved 11.9 billion cubic feet per day (Bcf/d), mostly to Asia and Europe, in 2024.2 For perspective, the US consumed 90 Bcf/d of natural gas, excluding exports, in 2024.3

Biggest getting bigger

The increase in demand through 2030 is supported by the 12 LNG export projects currently under construction.4 There has also been a significant increase in LNG contracting activity, with more than 3.0 Bcf/d of contracts signed and another 1.7 Bcf/d under consideration.5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10 Some of these will support the projects under construction, but also the 14 LNG projects that are considered “probable” but haven’t reached a final investment decision (FID). They could provide an additional 17 Bcf/d of export capacity.

Demand drivers

The US is likely to see a decade-long step up in power demand. It’s a global phenomenon, too. In 2024, data centers consumed 373 terawatt-hours of electricity globally, with 38% of that from the US. By 2035, it’s expected that data centers will consume 1,596 terawatt-hours of electricity.11 This quadrupling in demand is estimated to be mostly powered by natural gas, with a twelvefold growth in global combined cycle gas turbine and gas peaker demand from 2025 to 2035.11

In fact, despite the substantial growth in US LNG export capacity, it’s likely that demand will outpace supply in the next decade, based on operating and under-construction LNG facilities.12 While power generation demand plays a large role in this estimate, demand is also being driven by global switching from coal to gas, increasing use of natural gas for transportation in shipping and trucking, and, increasingly, industrialization.

Bring in the pipelines

The US can support this magnitude of LNG growth for two reasons. First, since 2011, it has been the largest producer of natural gas in the world. In 2023, it produced nearly double the natural gas of its closest competitor, Russia.13 Second, the US has the existing midstream infrastructure and the capability to build more facilities to meet the growing global demand.

Almost all of the currently operating and under-construction US LNG export facilities are along the Texas Gulf Coast and in Louisiana. While there’s existing significant natural gas infrastructure there, more pipelines are needed to move natural gas from the Permian Basin, Haynesville Shale, and other shale basins to meet the area’s natural gas needs. These recently completed or currently under construction pipelines are expected to move more than 13 Bcf/d of natural gas and help meet the demand:

  • Blackcomb Pipeline: Designed to move 2.5 bcf/d from the Permian Basin to the Agua Dulce area of south Texas, it’s expected to be in service in the second half of 2026.
  • Eiger Pipeline: Designed to move 2.5 bcf/d from the Permian Basin to the Katy, Texas area near Houston, it’s expected to be in service in mid-2028.
  • Trident Pipeline: Designed to move 2.0 Bcf/d from Katy, Texas to near Port Arthur, Texas, it’s expected to be in service in the first quarter of 2027.
  • Rio Bravo Pipeline: Designed to move 4.5 Bcf/d from the Agua Dulce Hub in south Texas to Next Decade’s Rio Grade LNG project in Brownsville, Texas, it’s expected to be in service in 2026.
  • Louisiana Energy Gateway: Designed to move 1.8 Bcf/d from the Haynesville Basin to south Louisiana, it began service in July 2025.

LNG demand-pull will benefit more than just the large, long-distance pipelines. The entire midstream logistics chain from wellhead gathering to processing, treating, compression, and storage is required to complete the movement of natural gas from producing basins to facilities. To meet this demand, new assets will need to be constructed. Even better, in some cases, existing assets with spare capacity may benefit from new capital. The growing LNG-driven demand should be healthy for many midstream providers.

Outlook

The midstream sector offers investors an attractive distribution yield and an improving outlook for cash flow and distribution growth, in our view. These fundamentals are supported by our expectation of significant natural gas volume growth for many years. Roughly 75% of the midstream sector is focused on natural gas production.14 Plus, natural gas demand appears set to potentially grow 25%–34% by the end of the decade, along with growing visibility into an additional 10% of demand growth from recent LNG commitments.15

While US natural gas demand experienced significant growth over the previous two decades, today’s demand growth is remarkable for its visibility and that it’s occurring in an environment of upstream and midstream capital discipline. As a result, we believe today’s natural demand growth may be particularly healthy for midstream investors.

Footnotes

1 Source: Invesco, “Midstream energy to fuel growth in AI,” Sept. 20, 2024.

2 Source: U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA), “The United States remained the world’s largest liquefied natural gas exporter in 2024,” March 27, 2025.

3 Source: US Energy Information Administration (EIA), ”U.S. natural gas consumption set new winter and summer monthly records in 2024,” March 31, 2025.

4 Source: Wells Fargo, “Equity Research – Weekender,” July 25, 2025.

5 The contracts represent 22 million tonnes per annum (MTPA) and 12 MPTA, respectively.

6 Source: Wolfe Research, “Midstream of Consciousness,” June 29, 2025.

7 Source: Cheniere Energy, Inc. (LNG), “Cheniere and JERA Sign Long-Term LNG Sale and Purchase Agreement,” Aug. 7, 2025.

8 Source: MarketWatch, “ConocoPhillips Signs 20-Year LNG Contract With Sempra,” Aug. 21, 2025.

9 Source: Reuters, “EQT to buy LNG from Sempra's Port Arthur Phase 2 project,” Aug. 27, 2025.

10 Source: EQT Corporation, “EQT Signs 20-Year Deal with NextDecade for 1.5 MTPA of LNG from Rio Grande LNG Train 5,” Sept. 5, 2025.

11 Source: Bloomberg L.P., BloombergNEF Theme: “AI Data Centers Fuel Quicker Growth in Power Demand,” Aug. 18, 2025.

12 Source: Shell, “LNG Outlook 2025,” Feb 2025.

13 Source: US Energy Information Administration (EIA), Aug. 2025.

14 Source: Bloomberg L.P., as of Aug. 27, 2025.

15 Source: KinderMorgan, assuming 28 Bcf/d natural gas demand growth.
